title: Layering matte and glossy glazes
subtitle: Dipping a matte base then applying a second glossy glaze in places for surface contrast and interaction
excerpt: Dip bisqueware in a matte base glaze, allow to dry completely, then apply a second glossy glaze by dipping or brushing in selected areas. The two glazes interact where they overlap and produce surface contrast between matte, glossy, and blended zones. Cone 6 (1220 degrees C) electric kiln.

glossaryTerms.definition: The first kiln firing (cone 06, approx 1000 degrees C) that hardens greenware into porous bisqueware
Pyrometric measure of heat-work in a kiln. Cone 06 approx 1000 degrees C, cone 6 approx 1220 degrees C, cone 10 approx 1280 degrees C
The second kiln firing that melts the glaze into a glassy coating and vitrifies the clay body; for cone 6 stoneware this occurs at approximately 1220 degrees C

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only: no sweeping, no dry sanding indoors.

Both matte and glossy glazes contain silica and fluxes. Handle any dry glaze components with gloves and a respirator. Pre-mixed glazes in jars are safer to use than dry powder mixes but still require wet-clean practices.
Ensure adequate ventilation when firing: a downdraft vent or open window with an extractor. Do not open a hot kiln below 80 degrees C. Keep a Class A fire extinguisher within reach.
Matte and glossy glazes interact differently at their overlap zone from how either behaves alone. Where a glossy glaze is applied over a dry matte base, the two layers melt together during the
glaze firing

to produce an intermediate surface: neither fully matte nor fully glossy, with colour blending and surface texture changes that are impossible to achieve with a single glaze. The three-zone surface (matte only, overlap, glossy only) gives the piece a dynamic, handmade quality. This technique requires a base glaze and an overglaze that are chemically compatible at
cone
6 (1220 degrees C). Both should be rated for the same cone range. The most reliable starting point is two glazes from the same manufacturer's cone 6 line.

materials.name: Mid-fire matte base glaze, pre-mixed
materials.quantity: enough to dip the full piece

materials.name: Mid-fire glossy overglaze, pre-mixed (contrasting colour or same colour for surface-only contrast)

tools.name: Hake brush (for softer, wider overglaze passes)
Pottery calipers (for measuring consistent dip depth)

content.text: Choosing the two glazes

The most predictable results come from two glazes that share the same cone rating and come from the same product range. Glazes from different manufacturers at the same cone rating can be layered, but the interaction in the overlap zone is harder to predict without a test fire. For a first attempt, use a plain pale matte base (cream, grey, or white) and a semi-gloss or full-gloss in a complementary tone. The colour contrast between the matte and glossy zones makes the layering effect visible even if both glazes are close in hue.
Applying the matte base
Wipe the
bisque
ware with a clean damp sponge and allow to dry for ten minutes. Stir the matte base glaze thoroughly.

Dip the piece into the matte base glaze for three seconds. Hold it by the foot-ring or by pinching the inside with two fingers. Remove, allow the drips to stop, and set it right-way-up on the banding wheel. Any drip lines will even out as the glaze dries into the bisque. Alternatively, brush the matte base in three even coats with the mop brush, allowing each to dry.

Clean any glaze from the foot-ring with a damp sponge. Allow the matte base coat to dry completely to a chalky finish: usually 20 to 30 minutes in a dry studio. The base must be fully dry before the overglaze is applied.
Applying the glossy overglaze

Decide where the glossy overglaze will appear: the upper half of the form, a band across the middle, a dripped area from the rim, or a broad brushed area on one face. Mark the boundary lightly in pencil if needed.

Dip method: lower only the chosen zone of the piece into the glossy glaze bucket for three seconds. The optional calipers can be used to mark a consistent dip depth on multiple pieces. Dipping gives the most even overlap band.

Brush method: load the mop brush with the glossy overglaze and apply in two to three coats with the banding wheel turning slowly. The overlap zone between the matte and glossy areas should have approximately 15 mm of coverage where the two glazes sit on top of each other. This overlap zone is where the most interesting surface interaction occurs.

Do not apply the glossy glaze to the foot-ring. Check that the total combined glaze thickness at the overlap zone is not excessive: two glaze layers should be no thicker than 2 to 3 mm total before firing. Thick combined layers run during firing.
Firing

Load the piece on a kiln shelf with adequate clearance from other pieces: 30 mm minimum, as the combination glaze may run slightly at the overlap zone. Fire to cone 6 (1220 degrees C) using a standard electric kiln schedule. Allow the kiln to cool fully. Do not open a hot kiln below 80 degrees C.
Reading the fired result

After firing, the piece shows three distinct zones: the matte base where no overglaze was applied; the overlap zone where the combined glaze has produced an intermediate surface with blended colour and mixed surface quality; and the glossy overglaze zone where only the second glaze is present. The overlap zone often shows a softened, slightly pooled edge where the glossy glaze melted into the matte base. This transition edge is the visual payoff of the technique.
Variations

Reversed layering: dip the glossy glaze first, let it dry, then apply the matte glaze by brushing in the lower half. The matte over glossy combination produces a different kind of interaction where the matte glaze can appear satin or slightly pulled.
Multiple dips: dip a second time in the glossy glaze slightly shallower than the first, creating two glaze bands with two distinct overlap zones.
Iron oxide accent: brush a line of iron oxide wash along the overlap zone before applying the overglaze. The iron will darken through the combined glaze layer and mark the transition edge.

rows.problem: Glaze ran and pooled at the base during firing
rows.cause: Combined glaze layers too thick at the overlap zone, or one of the glazes was inherently fluid at cone 6
rows.fix: Keep the combined layer no thicker than 2 to 3 mm. Apply the overglaze in one to two coats only rather than three. Test new combinations on tiles before using on finished pieces.

rows.problem: No visible difference between the two zones after firing
rows.cause: Both glazes fired to a similar surface quality, or the overglaze coat was too thin to affect the matte base surface
rows.fix: Choose glazes with a stronger matte-to-gloss contrast. Apply the overglaze in two full coats within the target zone.

rows.problem: Matte base coat lifted when the overglaze brush was applied
rows.cause: The matte base was not fully dry before the overglaze was brushed on
rows.fix: Allow the matte base to dry for at least 30 minutes before brushing the overglaze. Use a single-stroke brush motion over each area of the first overglaze coat rather than scrubbing back and forth.
